title: Burns Baby Burns

jokes.text: What a perfect outing for a beautiful autumn day. I feel sorry for everyone who's cooped up inside... watching the seventh game of the World Series. Yeah, they won't learn anything about apples today.
jokes.setup: Family is on educational apple farm trip instead of watching major sporting event
jokes.punchline: Dismissing World Series as less educational than apple facts
jokes.explanation: Solid character-driven irony about educational priorities over entertainment, typical Simpson family dynamic

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Children, isn't this educational? We're learning all about apples!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Did you know apples have been cultivated for over 4,000 years? The World Series is merely a sporting event.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Exactly! This is far more enriching than watching baseball.

jokes.text: The cider mill operated continuously until 1941... when its workers left to fight in the Second World War. When they returned, the old girl was just as they'd left her... only now she was infested with thousands upon thousands of rats.
jokes.setup: Nostalgic historical narrative about mill continuity
jokes.punchline: Dark reveal about rat infestation during wartime abandonment
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection from wholesome history to disturbing reality, well-structured buildup

dialogue.speaker: Tour Guide
dialogue.line: And this mill has been in continuous operation since 1887, through wars, depressions, and economic downturns...

dialogue.speaker: Tour Guide
dialogue.line: During World War II, when it was briefly abandoned, we had a little rat problem. By 'little,' I mean the rats were huge and they ate all the grain. In fact, some say the rats never really left...

jokes.text: Right. And if you listen real carefully... you can still hear them gnawing away at the apples... and splashing around in the toilets.
jokes.setup: Previous rat infestation revelation
jokes.punchline: Escalating disgusting details about ongoing rat presence
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the previous joke, 'splashing around in toilets' is particularly gross and effective

dialogue.speaker: Tour Guide
dialogue.line: Well, we had a little rat problem, but we got rid of them.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's great!

dialogue.speaker: Tour Guide
dialogue.line: Actually, we didn't. They're still here. In fact, they've nested in the walls and are chewing through the electrical wiring.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Oh my!

dialogue.speaker: Tour Guide
dialogue.line: And they've contaminated the water supply. Plus, we're pretty sure they're in the ventilation system now.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: This is horrifying.

dialogue.speaker: Tour Guide
dialogue.line: And last Tuesday, one of them was found in the deep fryer.

jokes.text: Well, if God didn't make little green apples, it's Homer Simpson.
jokes.setup: Traditional greeting expression about green apples
jokes.punchline: Barney adapting the phrase to recognize Homer at apple farm
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay perfectly suited to Barney's character and the apple farm setting

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: How do you like them green apples?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hey Barney!

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: How do you like them apples, Homer?

jokes.text: How long have you been here? - Twenty of the suckiest minutes of my life.
jokes.setup: Simple question about duration at educational venue
jokes.punchline: Barney's characteristically blunt assessment of educational experience
jokes.explanation: Perfect Barney response - direct, crude but honest about hating educational activities

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hey Barney, how long have you been going to that adult education class?

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: Oh, about three years now.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Three years? What have you learned?

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: That I'm not smart enough to be there.

jokes.text: Oh, sucking down the cider, huh? Hey, word to the wise- season pass. Pays for itself after the 16th visit.
jokes.setup: Homer offering practical advice about visiting apple farm
jokes.punchline: Absurdly specific number of visits needed to justify season pass
jokes.explanation: Excellent Homer logic - treating apple farm like an amusement park with precise economic calculation

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You know, if you're gonna visit an apple farm more than 47 times a year, you might as well get the season pass. It pays for itself after the 48th visit.

jokes.text: If it's clear and yella, you've got juice there, fella. If it's tangy and brown, you're in cider town.
jokes.setup: Homer explaining difference between apple juice and cider
jokes.punchline: Ridiculous rhyming mnemonic device
jokes.explanation: Perfectly crafted Homer wisdom - catchy rhyme, confident delivery, completely unnecessary complexity for simple concept

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Apple juice and cider? I'll tell you the difference. One is clear, one is cloudy. And one has a rhyme to help you remember: A-C-I-D makes cider!

jokes.text: And of course in Canada, the whole thing's flip-flopped.
jokes.setup: Homer's already complex cider/juice explanation
jokes.punchline: Adding completely random international complexity
jokes.explanation: Great escalation of the cider joke - Homer adding unnecessary international dimension to simple fruit drinks

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: It's like I always say: you take the cider, you ferment it, you age it in oak barrels for three years, you add a splash of Swedish lingonberry juice, a pinch of Himalayan sea salt, and a whisper of Peruvian wind chimes for that authentic terroir... and boom, you got yourself a refreshing glass of apple juice.

jokes.text: Just look at all this beautiful 'foilage.' - It's not 'foilage,' Mom. It's foliage. Foliage. - That's what I said- 'foilage.'
jokes.setup: Marge mispronouncing 'foliage'
jokes.punchline: Marge insisting she said it correctly while repeating mispronunciation
jokes.explanation: Classic Marge malapropism with good mother-daughter dynamic, Lisa's correction vs Marge's stubbornness

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: I was admiring all the beautiful foilage this fall.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Mom, it's pronounced 'foliage.'

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: That's what I said — foilage.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: No, foliage.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Foilage.

jokes.text: It's not 'foilage,' Mom. It's foliage. Foliage.
jokes.setup: Lisa correcting Marge's pronunciation
jokes.punchline: Sets up for Marge's continued mispronunciation
jokes.explanation: Good setup for the payoff - Lisa's pedantic correction

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Mom, it's pronounced 'quinoa,' not 'keen-wah.'

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Oh, well thank you, Lisa. I'll make sure to pronounce it correctly from now on.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Now, who wants some keen-wah for dinner?

jokes.text: It doesn't take a 'nucular' scientist to pronounce 'foilage.'
jokes.setup: Marge defending her pronunciation ability
jokes.punchline: Mispronouncing 'nuclear' while claiming pronunciation expertise
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation - Marge undermining her own pronunciation argument with another mispronunciation

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: I know how to pronounce words correctly.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: It's pronounced 'nucular,' not 'nuclear.'

jokes.text: Honestly, Smithers, I don't know why Harvard even bothers to show up. They barely even won. Their cheating was even more rampant than last year, sir.
jokes.setup: Burns commenting on Harvard's victory
jokes.punchline: Dismissing their win and accusing them of cheating despite winning
jokes.explanation: Good Burns snobbery - can't admit Yale lost fairly, classic entitled rich person logic

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: Harvard may have won, but I'm certain they cheated. No one beats my team without resorting to underhanded tactics.

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: But sir, Harvard won fair and square.

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: Nonsense, Smithers. Their victory is hollow. Mark my words, there will be consequences.

jokes.text: Why, every friend I have, I made right here. - Hello, Burnsie. It's your old roomy, Dink.
jokes.setup: Burns boasting about Yale friendships
jokes.punchline: Immediate appearance of annoying former roommate Burns clearly dislikes
jokes.explanation: Good timing joke - Burns' boast immediately contradicted by unwanted friend arrival

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: I have many fond memories of my time at Yale. Why, just last week I was reminiscing about my dear friend...

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: Hey Burnsie! Remember me? Your old roommate from Yale!

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Oh God, no.

jokes.text: Oh, this may take a while, Smithers. Why don't you get drunk and stumble around comically for my amusement? I'll be a one-man conga line.
jokes.setup: Train delay announcement
jokes.punchline: Burns treating Smithers like personal entertainment while planning own absurd dance
jokes.explanation: Excellent Burns-Smithers dynamic - casual cruelty combined with absurd self-entertainment plan

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: Smithers, due to the train delay, you'll have to entertain me. Do your little dance.

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: But sir, I don't really—

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: Now, Smithers! And make it lively!

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Very well, sir.

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: Bah! Dreadful! Now watch as I demonstrate the Charleston mixed with modern interpretive dance and a touch of the forbidden Transylvanian jig I learned from a gypsy.

jokes.text: I'll be a one-man conga line.
jokes.setup: Burns asks Smithers to drunkenly entertain him
jokes.punchline: Smithers enthusiastically agrees with specific absurd plan
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurd image - perfect economy and character moment

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Smithers, I'm bored. Entertain me.

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Of course, sir! I shall perform an interpretive dance while reciting the periodic table backwards, standing on one leg, while you throw eggs at me!

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Smithers, you're drunk.

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Yes, sir. And I'm only getting more enthusiastic!

jokes.text: Ah, customers. Thank God. Papa needs a new pair of everything.
jokes.setup: Desperate vendor seeing potential customers
jokes.punchline: Escalating gambling phrase 'pair of shoes' to 'pair of everything'
jokes.explanation: Good twist on classic gambling phrase showing complete desperation of roadside vendor

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: Hey buddy, you look like you could use a pair of shoes!

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: A pair of shoes? I'll take a pair of pants!

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: A pair of pants? How about a pair of socks?

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: A pair of socks? I'll take a pair of everything!

jokes.text: And remember your visit with a googly-eyed walnut. How about a googly-eyed rock? Some nice local squash candy?
jokes.setup: Vendor pitching tourist souvenirs
jokes.punchline: Increasingly pathetic items with googly eyes as 'souvenirs'
jokes.explanation: Good absurd escalation of terrible souvenir quality, googly eyes on random objects

dialogue.speaker: Vendor
dialogue.line: Hey, wanna buy a genuine Springfield souvenir?

dialogue.speaker: Vendor
dialogue.line: How about this brick with googly eyes?

dialogue.speaker: Vendor
dialogue.line: Or this used chewing gum with googly eyes?

dialogue.speaker: Vendor
dialogue.line: What about this pile of dirt with googly eyes?

dialogue.speaker: Tourist
dialogue.line: That's just googly eyes on nothing...

dialogue.speaker: Vendor
dialogue.line: Googly eyes are extra.

jokes.text: A stressed-out Pepsi bottle? Come on. If this stuff is too nice for you, I've got some crap.
jokes.setup: Already showing terrible souvenirs
jokes.punchline: Offering literal crap as lower tier option, admitting current items are 'too nice'
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation - vendor's desperation leads to offering actual garbage as merchandise tier system

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: These souvenirs are too nice. How about some literal crap instead?

jokes.text: I can honestly say that was the most fun I've ever had.
jokes.setup: Homer's reaction to educational apple farm visit
jokes.punchline: Claiming extreme enjoyment of something he clearly hated
jokes.explanation: Basic sarcasm, somewhat predictable given Homer's earlier complaints about the trip

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: An apple farm! Why, I had the time of my life! Best day ever!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, you spent the entire time complaining about the dirt and refusing to pick anything.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well sure, but I'm lying now.

jokes.text: I can't 'exscape' Lisa, our little walking 'libary.'
jokes.setup: Homer trying to be intellectual about Lisa's knowledge
jokes.punchline: Mispronouncing 'escape' and 'library' while praising literacy
jokes.explanation: Good Homer malapropism - undermining his own attempt to sound educated about education

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's great, Lisa. You know, I'm all for the 'scaype' and the 'libary'. Literacy is important.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, it's 'escape' and 'library.'

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's what I said.

jokes.text: Can't they get a pole for that sign? - That's a hitchhiker, Homer.
jokes.setup: Homer seeing hitchhiker from distance
jokes.punchline: Mistaking human for poorly designed signage
jokes.explanation: Excellent Homer obliviousness - seeing hitchhiker as infrastructure problem rather than person

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh boy, a hitchhiker! I'll pick him up.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait... is that a person or some kind of badly made road sign?

jokes.text: Ooh, let's pick him up. - No. What if he's crazy? - And what if he's not? Then we'd look like idiots.
jokes.setup: Marge's safety concern about hitchhiker
jokes.punchline: Homer's twisted logic about appearing foolish for helping normal person
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic inversion - making helping someone seem embarrassing rather than dangerous. Brilliant character writing.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, why would you pick up a hitchhiker? He could be dangerous!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Marge, if I don't pick up hitchhikers, people will think I'm not a good samaritan. But if I do pick up a hitchhiker and he turns out to be a normal person, then I'll look like an idiot for being nice to someone normal.

jokes.text: Man, it was rough getting a ride out there. The only car that stopped was a hearse that thought I fell out.
jokes.setup: Larry explaining difficulty hitchhiking
jokes.punchline: Only ride offer came from morticians assuming he was a corpse
jokes.explanation: Dark but clever joke about Larry's disheveled appearance and hitchhiking struggles

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I tried hitchhiking once. The only ride offer I got was from morticians who thought I was a corpse.

jokes.text: Careful of the apple pie on the seat. - Uh-oh. Grampa, are you sitting on the pie? - I sure hope so.
jokes.setup: Warning about pie placement with Grampa in car
jokes.punchline: Grampa preferring to sit on pie rather than alternative bathroom accident
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark elderly humor - Grampa's incontinence issues making pie destruction the better scenario

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Dad, please don't sit on the pie.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: Well, what else am I supposed to sit on?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Your ass?

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: I'm 81 years old. At this point, I'd rather sit on a pie.

jokes.text: Yeah, sure, we know him. That's Mr. Burns. - He tried to kill our puppies. - He sexually harassed me. - He stole my fiancée. - He made fun of my weight.
jokes.setup: Larry asking about Burns
jokes.punchline: Escalating catalog of Burns' horrible actions to each family member
jokes.explanation: Good escalating list of Burns villainy, callback to his various schemes and cruelty

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: So tell me about your boss, Mr. Burns.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, he's a pretty great guy. Sure, he tried to kill me once, but who hasn't?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, Mr. Burns tried to kill you multiple times!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah, but that was just business.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, the guy literally shot at us from his office window.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: And he's responsible for the nuclear plant's countless environmental violations that poison our town.

dialogue.speaker: Maggie
dialogue.line: (sucks pacifier)

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: See? Even the baby doesn't care. Mr. Burns is a swell employer.

jokes.text: Okay, so there's been a little friction. Know his address?
jokes.setup: Long list of Burns' terrible crimes
jokes.punchline: Larry dismissing serious accusations as minor 'friction'
jokes.explanation: Good understatement joke - treating sexual harassment and attempted puppy murder as minor social tension

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Well, yes, I may have done some terrible things, but they were all in the past.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Mr. Burns, you've committed murder, fraud, environmental crimes, and countless other felonies.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Pish posh! A little friction between business associates, that's all.

jokes.text: Whoa! This guy's got more bread than a prison meat loaf.
jokes.setup: Larry seeing Burns' mansion wealth
jokes.punchline: Comparing wealth to bread content in terrible prison food
jokes.explanation: Clever slang usage connecting 'bread' (money) to literal bread in prison food context

jokes.text: He's rich, I tell ya. I never seen a place with a walk-in mailbox.
jokes.setup: Larry commenting on Burns' wealth
jokes.punchline: Mailbox so large you can walk into it as sign of extreme wealth
jokes.explanation: Good absurd wealth indicator - treating mailbox like walk-in closet shows excessive scale

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: A mailbox so large you can walk into it — that's how you know someone's really rich.

jokes.text: Well, I hate to break it to you, Larry... but if Mr. Burns ever wants to see a stranger... he will observe him through a powerful telescope.
jokes.setup: Larry wanting to meet Burns
jokes.punchline: Burns' antisocial wealth requiring telescope for human contact
jokes.explanation: Good Burns characterization through Smithers - extreme wealth leads to extreme antisocial behavior

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Mr. Burns would be delighted to meet you, Larry. He's just finishing his afternoon constitutional.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Smithers, who is this ruffian? And why must he stand so close? I'll need my telescope just to see his face properly.

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Sir, he's standing right in front of you.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Precisely my point. The man is practically touching me. Fetch the periscope, Smithers. I refuse to conduct business at such intimate distances.

jokes.text: Oh, and I stepped on one of your peacocks. You got a paper towel?
jokes.setup: Larry delivering major emotional news about being Burns' son
jokes.punchline: Casually mentioning killing exotic pet as minor cleanup issue
jokes.explanation: Excellent comedic timing - deflating emotional revelation with casual mention of pet murder and cleanup request

dialogue.speaker: Larry Burns
dialogue.line: So it turns out I'm Mr. Burns' son. Yeah, we just found out.

dialogue.speaker: Larry Burns
dialogue.line: Anyway, I killed his albino peacock. It got loose in the house, so I had to take care of it.

jokes.text: It was 1941, the- No, '39, my 25th Yale reunion.
jokes.setup: Burns starting to tell story
jokes.punchline: Self-correction revealing his extreme age
jokes.explanation: Shows Burns' age through casual date confusion

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: I remember when they first invented the wheel. I said, 'What'll they think of next?' Ha! Well, that was in 1889.

jokes.text: I took Lily to the local 'cinematorium'... where our passions were inflamed by Clark Gable's reckless use of the word 'damn.'
jokes.setup: Burns narrating romantic evening
jokes.punchline: Old-fashioned sensibilities treating 'damn' as scandalously arousing
jokes.explanation: Good period humor about censorship and Burns' ancient perspective on what's scandalous

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: A romantic evening with Miss Smithers. I've prepared a special dinner, and I've even rented a film—a scandalous French picture where a woman says 'damn.' Arousing!

jokes.text: Of course by then, Mimsie had her share of wrinkles... and a gray hair or two, but my adoring eyes saw past those minor imperfections... to her 21-year-old daughter, Lily.
jokes.setup: Burns describing looking past Mimsie's age
jokes.punchline: Reveals he was actually interested in her young daughter
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection revealing Burns' shallow nature

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: I can look past Mimsie's age. What I'm really interested in is her daughter.

jokes.text: There, under the smiling eyes of four stuffed Eskimos... we expressed our love physically... as was the style at the time.
jokes.setup: Burns describing romantic encounter location
jokes.punchline: Bizarre museum setting with ethnic display and treating sex as fashion trend
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurd romanticism - museum diorama as love nest plus Burns' detached historical perspective

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: It was in the Hall of Ethnic Peoples at the Museum of Natural History. We made love right there among the dioramas.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: It was quite exhilarating — all those painted backdrops, the fake rocks, the wax figures staring down at us in judgment.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Though I must say, treating intercourse as a fleeting fashion trend seemed rather gauche, even for her.

jokes.text: Well, how do you like that? I have been in a museum.
jokes.setup: Burns describing passionate museum encounter
jokes.punchline: Homer's proud realization about his own minimal cultural exposure
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer response - missing emotional story completely, focusing on personal museum attendance milestone

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: I once made love to a woman on the hood of a Vermeer.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I've never even made love on a car.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: It was a Vermeer.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I know, I'm just saying I've never even made love on a car.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Well, the point is, I got art all over my ass.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, I've got Cheeto dust in my ass crack, so we're both pretty cultured.

jokes.text: Oh, what's to tell? I was at the orphanage till I was 18. Then I got my job at the souvenir stand. Oh, and once I saw a blimp.
jokes.setup: Burns asking Larry to tell him about his entire life
jokes.punchline: Extremely brief summary treating blimp sighting as major life event
jokes.explanation: Excellent contrast between request scope and response brevity - blimp as highlight of mundane life

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Tell me about your entire life.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Well, I was born, I saw a blimp once, and here we are.

jokes.text: You can't just eat the orange and throw the peel away! A man's not a piece of fruit! This show was supposed to close last week! Close this show! Close it, I say.
jokes.setup: Private theater performance in Burns' house
jokes.punchline: Actor breaking character to complain about show quality and demanding closure
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist theater humor - actor's professional breakdown during terrible private performance

dialogue.speaker: Theater Actor
dialogue.line: I can't do this anymore! This script is terrible, the lighting is atrocious, and I haven't been paid in three weeks!

dialogue.speaker: Theater Actor
dialogue.line: I demand closure on this production, or I'm walking off this stage right now!

jokes.text: Oh, relax. I don't wanna work. I'm so lazy I took lessons on a player piano.
jokes.setup: Larry reassuring Homer about not being work competition
jokes.punchline: Impossibly lazy scenario of taking lessons on self-playing instrument
jokes.explanation: Brilliant laziness joke - taking lessons on instrument that plays itself is perfectly absurd escalation

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Don't worry Homer, I'm not going to compete with you.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Really?

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Yeah, I'm taking lessons on the theremin.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: The what?

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: It's a musical instrument you play without touching it. You just wave your hands around.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's the laziest thing I've ever heard. I love it!

jokes.text: Wow! That's really lazy. Lazy, you're not kidding. Why, I'm like a rug on Valium. I'm talking lazy.
jokes.setup: Larry establishing his laziness
jokes.punchline: Escalating metaphor of already-stationary object on sedatives
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of laziness concept - rug on Valium is perfectly absurd immobility comparison

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I'm so lazy, I'm like a couch on tranquilizers.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Actually, I'm like a couch on tranquilizers that's already lying down.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: No wait—I'm like a couch on tranquilizers that's already lying down and just gave up.

jokes.text: And Larry and I have so much in common, Marge, way more than you and me. If I could be stranded on an island with anyone, it would definitely be Larry.
jokes.setup: Homer expressing enthusiasm for new friend
jokes.punchline: Preferring stranger over wife for survival scenario
jokes.explanation: Decent Homer marital insensitivity - preferring new friend over spouse in classic thoughtless way

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'd trust my new friend with my life!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: What about me?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, if we were on a sinking ship, I'd save him first.

jokes.text: Hey, Skipper, good to meet you. Where'd you start out, on the Merrimack? Hey, I should talk. I hope I look that good when I'm 200.
jokes.setup: Larry meeting Burns' distinguished friend
jokes.punchline: Crude maritime reference followed by age insult to elderly admiral
jokes.explanation: Good cringe humor - Larry's working-class directness clashing with upper-class social expectations

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Well, well, well... an admiral! You must have seen some action in your day.

dialogue.speaker: Admiral
dialogue.line: Indeed I have, my boy. Served with distinction for fifty years.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Fifty years? You must be older than the USS Constitution!

dialogue.speaker: Admiral
dialogue.line: How dare you!

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I've seen better preserved ships in a bottle.

jokes.text: Larry, you must meet our daughter, the debutante. She came out last spring. - Whoa! Put her back in! She's not done yet.
jokes.setup: Introducing debutante daughter using formal 'coming out' language
jokes.punchline: Larry misunderstanding as premature birth or cooking reference
jokes.explanation: Good wordplay on 'came out' social terminology vs. literal interpretation as incomplete process

dialogue.speaker: Society Woman
dialogue.line: I'm so pleased to announce that my daughter is coming out this season.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Coming out? But she's not due for three more months!

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Or did you mean she's done? Is she medium rare?

jokes.text: Pull up a seat, Pop. You too, Chuckles. I mean, the food ain't great, but the portions are terrific. Hey, I'm kidding. Relax. Hey, give my regards to Mrs. Boyardee.
jokes.setup: Larry commenting on fancy party food
jokes.punchline: Treating gourmet cuisine like casual dining with Chef Boyardee reference
jokes.explanation: Decent class clash humor - Larry's casual food references at formal gathering

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Wow, look at all this fancy food! Caviar, foie gras, truffles... You know what this reminds me of? Chef Boyardee ravioli. Same thing, really.

jokes.text: Oh, yes. He made light of my weight problem... then suggested my motto should be 'Semper Fudge.' At that point he told me to 'relax.'
jokes.setup: Yale rep describing Larry's inappropriate behavior
jokes.punchline: Military motto pun combining 'Semper Fi' with weight joke
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay on Marine Corps motto adapted for weight humor, good callback structure

dialogue.speaker: Yale Representative
dialogue.line: We can't admit Larry. He's too heavy.

dialogue.speaker: Yale Representative
dialogue.line: Our motto is 'Semper Fido' — always faithful. But Larry's always at the fridge.

jokes.text: Well, frankly, test scores like Larry's would call for a very generous contribution... he spelled 'Yale' with a six.
jokes.setup: Yale rep explaining Larry's academic challenges
jokes.punchline: Impossibly bad spelling of four-letter word
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurd academic failure - using number in place of letters shows extreme incompetence

dialogue.speaker: Yale Representative
dialogue.line: We've reviewed Larry's entrance exam, and I'm afraid his spelling is... well, look at how he spelled 'love'.

dialogue.speaker: Yale Representative
dialogue.line: L-U-V-V-V-V-V-V-V-V.

jokes.text: And in Larry's case, we would need an international airport. Yale could use an international airport, Mr. Burns. Are you mad? I'm not made of airports! Get out!
jokes.setup: Escalating donation requirements based on test scores
jokes.punchline: Burns rejecting airport donation with literal interpretation of wealth limits
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation joke - 'not made of airports' is brilliantly absurd wealth limitation statement

dialogue.speaker: Yale Representative
dialogue.line: Mr. Burns, your son's test scores are abysmal. We'll need a $5 million donation.

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: Five million? That's outrageous! What if I donated $3 million?

dialogue.speaker: Yale Representative
dialogue.line: Then he'd be rejected.

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: What about $10 million?

dialogue.speaker: Yale Representative
dialogue.line: That would guarantee his acceptance.

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: Excellent! I'll give you $7 million.

dialogue.speaker: Yale Representative
dialogue.line: Sir, that's between the two amounts. He'd be on the waiting list.

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: Then I'm not donating at all. I refuse to waste money on a school that can't make up its mind!

jokes.text: Are you mad? I'm not made of airports! Get out!
jokes.setup: Yale wanting airport donation for Larry's admission
jokes.punchline: Burns' literal interpretation and outrage
jokes.explanation: Perfect Burns response - literal and indignant about money limits

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Yale wants a donation for Larry's admission? Preposterous! I'll give them nothing!

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Sir, I believe they meant a monetary contribution to the university.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: A monetary contribution? Why didn't they say so? I thought they wanted an actual airport! Do I look like I build airports?

jokes.text: Okay, but the last guy who charged a drink to Burns turned up in a landfill. Yeah, but it was worth it.
jokes.setup: Warning about Burns' reaction to unauthorized charges
jokes.punchline: Casual acceptance that drink was worth murder risk
jokes.explanation: Good dark humor about Burns' murderousness and working-class priorities about free drinks

dialogue.speaker: Bartender
dialogue.line: That'll be $5 for the drink.

dialogue.speaker: Patron
dialogue.line: Five dollars?! Mr. Burns will kill me if he finds out I spent money without his permission.

dialogue.speaker: Bartender
dialogue.line: Well, it's a really good drink.

dialogue.speaker: Patron
dialogue.line: Worth it.

jokes.text: Hey, what's everybody in this burg have against my dad? He's a pussycat. I tell you, he's a doll-baby.
jokes.setup: Larry defending Burns after hearing murder threat
jokes.punchline: Describing known villain with childish affectionate terms
jokes.explanation: Good irony of Larry's innocent view contrasting with everyone else's Burns experience

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Sure, Mr. Burns is a bit unpleasant. He's always firing people, he tried to kill me that one time, but... he's not a murderer! He's just a scamp! A rascal! A cantankerous old coot!

jokes.text: Ah, this is some party. If it gets any livelier, a funeral's gonna break out.
jokes.setup: Larry commenting on dull Burns gathering
jokes.punchline: Inverting 'party breaking out' phrase with funeral as escalation
jokes.explanation: Good phrase inversion joke - treating funeral as more exciting than current social gathering

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: This is the dullest party I've ever been to. It's so boring, a funeral would break out.

jokes.text: You, food bag, do you have a son? Yes, sir, I do. And is he a constant disappointment? Does he bring home nitwits and make you talk to them? Oh, all the time. Have you ever heard of this kid Milhouse? He's a little wiener who-
jokes.setup: Burns seeking parental solidarity about disappointing children
jokes.punchline: Homer eagerly bonding over insulting Bart's best friend
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Homer's parental frustrations aligning with Burns' problems

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: Homer, I understand you also have a son who is a constant disappointment.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah, Bart's a real underachiever.

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: Perhaps we could commiserate. My boy is equally worthless.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You know what really gets me? Bart's best friend Milhouse. That kid's a real loser.

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: Ah yes, I quite agree.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I mean, his glasses are so thick, his head's so weak, and his family's so poor that—

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: Yes, yes, a truly pathetic specimen.

jokes.text: Oh, all the time. Have you ever heard of this kid Milhouse? He's a little wiener who-
jokes.setup: Burns asking about disappointing sons
jokes.punchline: Comic Book Guy's harsh assessment of Milhouse
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Comic Book Guy's harsh judgment

dialogue.speaker: Comic Book Guy
dialogue.line: Worst. Son. Ever.

jokes.text: Geez! Dad and I, we started out great... but now it's falling apart, like a Chinese motorcycle.
jokes.setup: Larry lamenting deteriorating relationship with Burns
jokes.punchline: Comparing relationship to stereotypically unreliable product
jokes.explanation: Decent but somewhat dated ethnic stereotype humor, relies on familiar comparison

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Our relationship has gotten worse than a Chevy from the 70s.

jokes.text: Yeah. I tell ya, I don't get no regard. No regard at all. No esteem either.
jokes.setup: Larry lamenting his situation
jokes.punchline: Rodney Dangerfield impression/reference
jokes.explanation: Straightforward celebrity impression without much originality

jokes.text: Larry, there's only one sure way to make him realize how much he loves you... and that is a phony kidnapping.
jokes.setup: Larry seeking advice about father relationship
jokes.punchline: Homer suggesting elaborate criminal scheme as family therapy
jokes.explanation: Excellent Homer logic - treating fake kidnapping as reasonable relationship advice

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Homer, I'm having trouble with my father. Do you have any advice?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, when I have problems with my dad, we do something together. Like robbing a bank or hijacking a plane.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: That's terrible advice!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: It works for us!

jokes.text: Your son has been kidnapped.
jokes.setup: Homer executing the fake kidnapping plan
jokes.punchline: Shows Homer writing ransom note despite being illiterate
jokes.explanation: Good setup for the kidnapping plot with Homer's characteristic incompetence

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Dear Mr. Burns, I have your son. If you don't pay me one million dollars, I will hurt him.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: P.S. I am not a crook.

jokes.text: All right, Mr. B., when the kidnappers call with the ransom demand... you tell them you'll leave the money under the big net in the park. And then down comes the net. Right, Chief?
jokes.setup: Police planning kidnapping response strategy
jokes.punchline: Cartoonishly simple trap plan using park equipment
jokes.explanation: Good absurd police competence - treating serious crime with slapstick cartoon logic

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Alright men, we got ourselves a kidnapping. Here's the plan: We'll set up a net made of police tape between these two trees.

dialogue.speaker: Eddie
dialogue.line: Chief, won't the kidnapper just go around it?

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Ah, good point. We'll also put up a sign that says 'Kidnapper trap - do not cross.'

dialogue.speaker: Lou
dialogue.line: What if he can read, Chief?

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Then we put the sign in French.

jokes.text: Hiding out? [Raspberry] Marge, you've been reading too many 'hide out' books.
jokes.setup: Marge suggesting Homer is hiding in basement
jokes.punchline: Homer creating fictional genre of 'hide out books' as explanation
jokes.explanation: Good Homer deflection technique - inventing absurd literature category to dismiss valid observation

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, you've been in the basement all day. What are you doing down there?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm not hiding, Marge. I'm reading hide-out books.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Hide-out books?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: It's a whole genre. There's 'Where's Waldo,' 'I Spy'... very educational.

jokes.text: He's great at pointing out everyone's foibles. Hey! How you doing? Ooh, look at your hair. What happened? You saw yourself in a mirror?
jokes.setup: Bart praising Larry's humor style
jokes.punchline: Larry immediately insulting Marge's hair with cruel observation
jokes.explanation: Decent cringe humor - demonstrating Larry's insult comedy style through immediate example

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Your humor style is amazing, Larry!

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Thanks, kid. Hey Marge, did you know that blue beehive makes you look like a sad Smurf had a baby with a haystack?

jokes.text: I just can't get the zoning permits.
jokes.setup: Larry claiming to want hair like Marge's
jokes.punchline: Treating tall hair as construction project requiring municipal approval
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurd comparison - treating Marge's signature hair height as municipal planning issue

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I want hair like Marge Simpson's.

dialogue.speaker: City Official
dialogue.line: You'll need to file a permit with the city. That's a structural modification.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: How long will that take?

dialogue.speaker: City Official
dialogue.line: Eight to ten weeks for the environmental impact study alone. We need to make sure it doesn't block anyone's view or violate zoning codes.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: It's just hair!

dialogue.speaker: City Official
dialogue.line: At that height, it's a landmark.

jokes.text: Hey, that reminds me, Mom. Buckingham Palace called. They want their hat back.
jokes.setup: Larry continuing hair-focused humor toward Marge
jokes.punchline: Comparing Marge's hair to British royal headwear
jokes.explanation: Decent insult comedy but fairly predictable royal comparison for unusual hair

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Hey Marge, with that hair, you could be the Queen of England!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Well, thank you!

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Yeah, you'd be perfect for a crown — it'd finally have something to sit on top of.

jokes.text: Ahoy, hoy?
jokes.setup: Burns answering telephone
jokes.punchline: Using historically accurate but outdated phone greeting
jokes.explanation: Perfect Burns characterization through period-appropriate but obsolete telephone etiquette

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Ahoy-hoy?

jokes.text: Yes, I'm missing one son. Return it immediately.
jokes.setup: Kidnapper asking if Burns misses his son
jokes.punchline: Burns treating son like lost property to be returned
jokes.explanation: Excellent Burns emotional detachment - treating kidnapped son as inventory management issue

dialogue.speaker: Kidnapper
dialogue.line: Don't you miss your son?

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: Of course. I'd give anything to have him back. Well, not *anything*. I'd give... let's say fifty dollars.

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: That's a fair price for a boy. Perhaps even a bit generous.

jokes.text: Whoa! How much proof do you need? Five thousand, six thousand? I swear, that's all I've got.
jokes.setup: Fake kidnapper asking Burns to prove he loves Larry
jokes.punchline: Burns misunderstanding emotional proof as financial payment
jokes.explanation: Perfect Burns character flaw - unable to conceive of non-monetary expressions of love

dialogue.speaker: Fake Kidnapper
dialogue.line: Prove you love your son! Show me some emotion!

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: Emotion? Why, I'd write a check right now.

jokes.text: Sure did, Chief. 555- Oh, geez, that's gotta be phony.
jokes.setup: Police tracing phone number from kidnapping call
jokes.punchline: Recognizing TV/movie fake phone number prefix
jokes.explanation: Good meta humor - police character recognizing fictional phone number convention from media

dialogue.speaker: Eddie
dialogue.line: We traced the call. It came from the number 555-0199.

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: 555? Isn't that the fake prefix they use in TV and movies?

dialogue.speaker: Eddie
dialogue.line: Yeah, Chief. That's the one.

jokes.text: Hey, I think I got a pai gow. [Groans] I hate pai gow! Can't we just play 'quang jong'?
jokes.setup: Homer and Larry playing cards while hiding
jokes.punchline: Homer making up Asian card game names with wrong pronunciation
jokes.explanation: Decent Homer cultural confusion - mangling Asian game names shows his typical ignorance

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Larry, what card game should we play?

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I don't know, Homer. What do you suggest?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: How about Pai Gow? No wait... Pie Cow. Or is it Poy Gow? Poy Cow?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What about Mahjong? Mah-jong? Muh-jonk? My-junk?

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Homer, just deal the cards.

jokes.text: Marge, it's not a real kidnapping. It's a simple hoax to win a father's love.
jokes.setup: Marge concerned about kidnapping
jokes.punchline: Homer's casual dismissal of serious crime as 'simple hoax'
jokes.explanation: Good Homer rationalization of criminal activity

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, I'm worried about kidnapping. What if someone took one of the children?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Marge, kidnapping is just a simple hoax made up by the media to scare us.

jokes.text: Marge, it's broad daylight, and there's cops everywhere. No excuses. Just do it.
jokes.setup: Marge demanding Homer return Larry to end fake kidnapping
jokes.punchline: Homer pointing out obviously impossible circumstances for return
jokes.explanation: Basic situational irony - Homer's rare moment of practical thinking ignored by demanding wife

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, you have to return Larry! This kidnapping has gone on long enough!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: But Marge, I can't return him. He's in a wooden crate at the bottom of the ocean.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Well then get him out!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I would, but I don't have any scuba gear, I can't hold my breath for more than three seconds, and I don't even know which ocean.

jokes.text: Appearing in broad daylight with police everywhere, ladies and gentlemen... there's only one word for that- idiocy.
jokes.setup: News reporter describing Homer and Larry's public appearance during manhunt
jokes.punchline: Blunt assessment of their obviously poor criminal planning
jokes.explanation: Good news anchor commentary - professional assessment of amateur criminal behavior

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: Well, it appears the two suspects are not very good at being on the lam. One of them is wearing a name tag.

jokes.text: D'oh! Stupid economic recovery.
jokes.setup: Homer seeking abandoned warehouse for hiding
jokes.punchline: Blaming economic improvement for lack of available abandoned buildings
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurd complaint - Homer cursing economic prosperity for interfering with criminal hiding spots

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Where am I gonna find an abandoned warehouse now? The economy's too good!

jokes.text: D'oh! Stupid economic recovery.
jokes.setup: Finding abandoned warehouse is now occupied
jokes.punchline: Homer blaming good economy for lack of abandoned buildings
jokes.explanation: Brilliant irony - complaining about economic improvement as inconvenience

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh, a perfectly good abandoned warehouse! And it's all because of this booming economy. Back in my day, you couldn't swing a dead cat without hitting an abandoned building. Now they're all fixed up and full of jobs and hope. It's ruining everything!

jokes.text: Yeah, this place is emptier than a Scottish pay toilet.
jokes.setup: Larry commenting on empty movie theater
jokes.punchline: Ethnic stereotype about Scottish frugality applied to public restrooms
jokes.explanation: Decent comparison joke relying on familiar ethnic stereotype about Scottish cheapness

jokes.text: Shh. Some people are trying to watch the movie. You don't like it, call a cop.
jokes.setup: Movie patron complaining about noise from fugitives
jokes.punchline: Suggesting calling police while talking to actual fugitive
jokes.explanation: Good situational irony - moviegoer unknowingly giving perfect advice about fugitives

dialogue.speaker: Movie Patron
dialogue.line: Excuse me, sir. Could you please keep it down? We're trying to watch the movie.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Why don't you call the police?

dialogue.speaker: Movie Patron
dialogue.line: Good idea!

jokes.text: Now, here's how it would look if the police killed him with a barrage of baseballs.
jokes.setup: News simulation of possible violent police action
jokes.punchline: Replacing bullets with baseballs in police violence scenario
jokes.explanation: Good dark absurdist humor - sanitizing police violence through sports equipment substitution

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: We now return to our simulation of police violence. Officers, you may fire at will.

dialogue.speaker: Police Officer
dialogue.line: We're out of bullets, Chief.

dialogue.speaker: Police Chief
dialogue.line: Use baseballs!

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: And that's how real police work.

jokes.text: All your son wanted was a little attention, a little love. I'm a father myself, sir. And, sure, sometimes my kids can be obnoxious... or boring, or stinky. But they can always count on one thing- their father's unconditional love.
jokes.setup: Homer giving Burns heartfelt speech about parental love
jokes.punchline: Including 'boring' and 'stinky' as casual child characteristics in love declaration
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Homer's sincere but tactless parenting philosophy includes unflattering honesty

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Mr. Burns, I know we've had our differences, but I want you to know that being a parent means loving your child no matter what. Even if they're boring and stinky, you love them anyway.

jokes.text: Oh, that's okay. I got a wife and kids. Oh, that reminds me. They're probably wondering where I went. I told them I'm going for coffee. That was a week ago.
jokes.setup: Larry accepting Burns' rejection casually
jokes.punchline: Revealing he abandoned his own family for a week over coffee excuse
jokes.explanation: Excellent character revelation - Larry's casual abandonment of family shows his own dysfunction

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Oh, that's okay, Mr. Burns. I understand completely.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Besides, I left my wife and kids at a rest stop for three days once because I thought I saw a Starbucks.

jokes.text: It's good to know that... there's another kidney out there for me.
jokes.setup: Burns expressing affection for newly discovered son
jokes.punchline: Reducing relationship to organ donation potential
jokes.explanation: Perfect Burns sentiment - turning parental love into practical medical planning, completely in character

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: I've discovered I have a son! Finally, a legacy to pass on to the next generation.

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: Why, with his healthy organs, I could live another 200 years!

jokes.text: You got it, Pop. Just let me run a few pints through it first.
jokes.setup: Burns seeing Larry as kidney source
jokes.punchline: Larry agreeing but requesting to use kidney for drinking first
jokes.explanation: Good continuation of kidney joke - Larry's alcoholism as kidney preparation process

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Excellent! I'll take one of your kidneys.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Well, okay, but I get to use it for drinking first.

jokes.text: It's a party, Marge. It doesn't have to make sense.
jokes.setup: Marge questioning spontaneous party logistics
jokes.punchline: Homer's philosophy about party logic exemption
jokes.explanation: Good meta commentary on sitcom logic - Homer dismissing plot consistency for celebration

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, how did you manage to throw together a party this fast? Where did you get all this food and decorations?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, Marge, you see, when you're throwing a party, the normal rules of logic don't apply. It's like a special exemption zone where anything goes and nobody questions how things happen.

tmdb_overview: Mr. Burns' boorish long-lost son Larry gets the brush-off from Burns, but Homer hatches a plan to reconcile them.
insight: Season 8's densest episode: 79 jokes in 23 minutes, anchored by Burns' illegitimate son arc.
